name: Nickel
name_origin: From the German 'Kupfernickel' ('Devil's copper' or 'Old Nick's copper'), the miners' name for niccolite (NiAs), an ore that resembled copper ore but yielded none.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Nickel is the 22nd-23rd most abundant element in Earth's crust (~84 ppm) but is the second most abundant element in Earth's core, after iron. Most commercial nickel comes from sulfide ores (pentlandite) in regions such as Sudbury, Canada and from laterite ores in tropical regions; iron meteorites also contain significant nickel.

compounds.name: Nickel(II) oxide
compounds.summary: A green solid used in ceramic glazes, ferrites, and as a catalyst precursor.

compounds.name: Nickel(II) chloride
compounds.summary: A yellow (anhydrous) or green (hydrated) salt used in electroplating baths and as a precursor to other nickel compounds.

compounds.name: Nickel(II) sulfate
compounds.summary: An emerald-green salt used extensively in nickel electroplating and as a mordant in dyeing.

compounds.name: Nickel tetracarbonyl
compounds.summary: A volatile, extremely toxic colorless liquid used in the Mond process to produce high-purity nickel.

compounds.name: Nickel oxyhydroxide
compounds.summary: The active cathode material in nickel-cadmium and nickel-metal-hydride rechargeable batteries.

compounds.name: Heazlewoodite
compounds.summary: A nickel sulfide mineral and a key intermediate in pyrometallurgical refining of nickel sulfide ores.

uses: Stainless steel and other corrosion-resistant alloys (~70% of nickel use)
Nickel-based superalloys (e.g., Inconel, Hastelloy) for jet engines and gas turbines
Cathodes in nickel-metal-hydride and lithium-ion (NMC, NCA) batteries
Electroplating to provide a corrosion-resistant decorative finish
Catalyst for hydrogenation of vegetable oils and many industrial reactions (e.g., Raney nickel)
Coinage metal in nickel-copper alloys (e.g., the U.S. five-cent coin)
reactivity_summary: Nickel is a silvery-white, lustrous, ferromagnetic transition metal with a Curie temperature of 631 K. It is fairly resistant to corrosion at room temperature due to a passive oxide film, but reacts slowly with dilute acids and is attacked by nitric acid. Finely divided nickel is pyrophoric and is widely used as a hydrogenation catalyst. The +2 oxidation state dominates aqueous chemistry; nickel is a recognized skin allergen and some compounds are carcinogenic.
isotopes_summary: Nickel has five stable isotopes: 58Ni (most abundant at ~68.08%), 60Ni, 61Ni, 62Ni, and 64Ni. 62Ni has the highest binding energy per nucleon of any nuclide. The radioisotope 63Ni (half-life 100 years) is used in electron-capture detectors for gas chromatography and in some beta-voltaic batteries.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
